items.title: Trump signs executive order reclassifying federal workforce positions
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order reclassifying about 8,000 senior policy-influencing federal positions into a new Schedule Policy/Career category.
items.details: The order reclassifies roughly 8,000 senior policy-influencing positions into Schedule Policy/Career. The White House states the roles remain career positions with non-partisan hiring processes unchanged, while removal decisions are to be made without respect to political affiliation. OPM later issued implementation guidance to agencies.

items.title: GAO reports federal workforce decline in 2025
items.summary: A GAO report found the civilian workforce at 22 major agencies declined by nearly 256,000 employees, or more than 11%, from December 2024 to January 2026.
items.details: GAO reported that nearly 378,000 employees separated from 22 major agencies during 2025, about 65% in the second half as deferred-resignation departures took effect, while about 127,000 were hired. Declines ranged from about 1% at Homeland Security to over 45% at the Department of Education.

items.title: White House issues executive order on AI cybersecurity
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order titled 'Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security' directing federal agencies to strengthen cyber defenses.
items.details: Issued June 2, 2026, the order directs agencies to strengthen government and private-sector cyber defenses against advanced AI and to develop voluntary benchmarking frameworks for frontier AI models. It establishes a voluntary frontier-model engagement framework and directs enforcement against AI-driven cybercrime.

items.title: U.S. and Iran sign memorandum of understanding to end war
items.summary: President Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian signed a memorandum of understanding laying out terms to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
items.details: The interim agreement, signed late June 17, starts a 60-day negotiating window over Iran's nuclear program. The memorandum states the Strait of Hormuz will reopen, the U.S. will lift sanctions and its naval blockade, and Iran reaffirms it will not develop nuclear weapons.

items.title: Markets react to U.S.-Iran framework agreement
items.summary: Oil prices fell and U.S. stocks rose after the United States and Iran announced an agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
items.details: Brent crude fell 4.76% to $83.17 per barrel and WTI fell 4.87% to $80.75, both lowest settle prices since March 4. The Dow gained 469 points to close at a record 51,671, while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq also rose sharply.

items.title: Senate passes immigration enforcement funding bill
items.summary: The Senate voted 52-47 to pass legislation funding Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol for three years.
items.details: The $70 billion bill funds ICE and Border Patrol through the end of the term. Senators rejected multiple amendments related to a separate settlement fund. The bill passed early June 5 after weeks of delays.

items.title: House passes Secure America Act
items.summary: The House passed S. 2, the Secure America Act, on a simple-majority vote on June 9.
items.details: The measure passed the House on June 9 following Senate passage on June 5. The vote was recorded as House Vote #214 in 2026 on final passage of the bill.

items.title: Section 702 surveillance authorization lapses
items.summary: FISA's Section 702 authorization lapsed during congressional activity in mid-June, though the program is expected to continue.
items.details: According to a legislative recap, many anti-fraud bills passed mostly by voice vote and DHS received additional funding through 2029, while FISA's Section 702 authorization lapsed. Analysts noted the program itself is likely to continue uninterrupted.

items.title: Supreme Court rules in United States v. Hemani
items.summary: The Supreme Court issued its decision in United States v. Hemani, a Second Amendment case involving a federal firearms restriction on unlawful drug users.
items.details: Argued March 2 and decided June 18, 2026, the case concerned Ali Hemani, a Texas man prosecuted under a statute barring firearm possession by unlawful drug users based on marijuana use. A federal appeals court had ruled in his favor; the Trump administration defended the restriction.
items.source: Supreme Court of the United States

items.title: Supreme Court allows Alabama to use redrawn congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court issued an unsigned 6-3 order allowing Alabama to use legislatively drawn maps, removing the prior injunction.
items.details: On June 2, 2026, the Court granted an emergency request to remove the Milligan injunction and allow use of the legislatively drawn maps, which removed a majority-Black district that had been added by court-drawn maps. The order was issued without a signed opinion.

items.title: Supreme Court enters final stretch of term with major cases pending
items.summary: The Supreme Court has 22 cases left to decide, including challenges over birthright citizenship and presidential removal of agency officials.
items.details: As of mid-June, 22 of 58 argued cases remained. The Court had already decided cases striking down the administration's tariff program and weakening the Voting Rights Act. Pending cases include Trump v. Barbara on birthright citizenship and disputes over independent agencies.

items.title: Louisiana enacts new congressional map after Supreme Court ruling
items.summary: Louisiana lawmakers passed and Governor Jeff Landry signed a new congressional map eliminating one majority-Black district following the Louisiana v. Callais ruling.
items.details: The Republican-controlled legislature approved a new map after the Supreme Court struck down the prior map. The new plan redraws Rep. Cleo Fields' district and adds part of Baton Rouge to Rep. Troy Carter's majority-Black district. Civil rights groups have criticized the plan and further litigation is expected.

items.title: Oklahoma holds primary; Alabama and Georgia hold runoffs
items.summary: Oklahoma held its 2026 primary on June 16, with primary runoffs in Alabama and Georgia and special elections in California and Washington, D.C.
items.details: Voters in Oklahoma cast ballots in congressional and statewide primaries, while Alabama and Georgia held primary runoffs. Additional contests included a California congressional district special election and races in Washington, D.C.

items.title: Georgia special session declines to take up redistricting
items.summary: Georgia House Republican leaders said they would not redraw congressional or legislative maps during the June 17 special session called by Governor Brian Kemp.
items.details: Kemp called the session after the Louisiana v. Callais ruling, citing a need for new maps before 2028. House Speaker Jon Burns said lawmakers need more time to understand the ruling and cited ongoing litigation, declining to take up redistricting while focusing on tax relief and the gas tax suspension.

CA.title: Acting Governor Limón signs local government bills
CA.summary: Acting Governor Monique Limón signed legislation modernizing local government operations, becoming the first Latina to sign laws as Acting Governor in California.
CA.details: On June 17, Limón signed bills including SB 1080 on county clerk record certification and several Committee on Local Government validation measures. The bills aim to modernize local government operations and reduce administrative burdens.
CA.source: Office of the Governor of California

NY.title: Kyra's Law delivered to Governor Hochul
NY.summary: New York's Kyra's Law, requiring judges to weigh child-safety allegations first in custody cases, passed both chambers and was delivered to Governor Hochul.
NY.details: The bill passed both chambers June 5 and reached Hochul June 15. It requires family and matrimonial judges to resolve credible domestic-violence and child-abuse allegations as a threshold safety question before weighing other custody factors. Hochul has until the end of 2026 to sign or veto.

PA.title: Governor Shapiro signs World Cup hours bill
PA.summary: Governor Josh Shapiro signed Senate Bill 1218, allowing Philadelphia bars and restaurants to stay open two extra hours during the FIFA World Cup and America250 celebrations.
PA.details: The law permits extended hours between June 11 and July 20, 2026, coinciding with the FIFA World Cup and the nation's 250th anniversary observances.

CO.title: Governor Polis signs fingerprint background check bill
CO.summary: Governor Jared Polis signed SB25-146 on June 2, allowing regulators to require fingerprint-based criminal history checks for certain licensed professions.
CO.details: The law allows regulators to require fingerprint-based criminal history record checks for applicants in specified professions, with applicants responsible for associated costs. The state described the change as aligning Colorado language with federal law.
CO.source: Colorado Division of Professions and Occupations

TX.title: Governor Abbott issues severe storm disaster proclamation
TX.summary: Governor Greg Abbott issued a disaster proclamation for dozens of Texas counties after severe storms beginning June 14, 2026.
TX.details: The proclamation cited severe storms including heavy rainfall, flash flooding, hazardous wind gusts, large hail, and tornado threats causing widespread damage across numerous counties. It authorizes suspension of certain rules to aid emergency response.

GA.title: Georgia special session opens without redistricting
GA.summary: Georgia lawmakers convened a special session June 17 but declined to redraw congressional or legislative maps, focusing instead on election and tax matters.
GA.details: Governor Kemp called the session following Louisiana v. Callais. House leaders said they would not take up redistricting, citing pending litigation and the need for public input, and directed the session toward tax relief and ratifying the gas tax suspension. Demonstrators gathered at the Capitol.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable recent developments from cited sources without endorsing any party, official, or position.
